textPlain: > After announcing its retirement, many generous people and organizations offered help - thank you! The project is now transitioning to an RIR (Regional Internet Registry), which will continue running the site in the public interest.well played.  threatening to turn it off on christmas was a great move, must've rattled a few cages in the industry lol.
I wonder which RIR it is. ARIN or RIPE?

It's an apparently-simple site, with a greater utility than might at first meet the eye.  I've found it very useful over the years, and I'm glad Jason has found someone he's happy to hand it over to.Thanks, Jason, all your efforts are much appreciated.
Meanwhile some ISPs are still not offering IPv6, such as Orange Belgium.
I use it to test connectivity in general. That it also checks whether my IPv6 stacks works correctly is basically a bonus.

> The project is now transitioning to an RIR (Regional Internet Registry)

RIR as in the orgs responsible for managing IP blocks? Huh?What does that actually mean in the context of a website like this?
less useful but vaguely relatedhttps://1.1.1.1/helpand even less usefullhttps://ipv6test.google.com
emphasis on "apparently simple"icanhazip.com looks even simpler on the face of it but there's always shenanigans behind the curtain:https://major.io/p/a-new-future-for-icanhazip/
RIRs also run public (web) infrastructure, and it sounds like one of them will take over this project. What's your question exactly?
